tasks.prompt: Apply both manifests to your cluster using a single `kubectl apply` command that targets the current directory, then confirm that all three Pods reach `Running` status with `READY 1/1`.
tasks.starter:
# Assume you are in the directory that contains deployment.yaml and service.yaml
# (created in Lecture 4, image flask-capstone:1.0 already loaded into the cluster)

# 1. Apply everything in the current folder
# 2. Watch Pod status until all are Running
tasks.explanation: `kubectl apply -f .` reads every YAML file in the current directory and sends each one's desired state to the Kubernetes API server as a declarative upsert — safe to run repeatedly. The `-w` (watch) flag on `get pods` streams status changes in real time so you can see Pods move from `Pending` → `ContainerCreating` → `Running` without polling manually. You are done when the READY column reads `1/1` for every Pod.
hints: Pointing `apply` at `.` (a directory) is equivalent to listing each YAML file individually; it is the idiomatic way to manage a multi-file workload.

If Pods stay in `Pending` for more than a minute, run `kubectl describe pod <name>` and look at the Events section at the bottom — it will tell you whether the scheduler cannot find a node or whether the image cannot be pulled.
Press Ctrl-C to exit the `-w` watch loop once all Pods show `Running`.

tasks.prompt: Use the three-command triage flow (`get` → `describe` → `logs`) to inspect your running Deployment. First list all Pods and the Service, then describe one Pod (note the Events section), then stream its logs live.
tasks.starter:
# Replace <pod-name> with an actual Pod name from your cluster
# Example: flask-capstone-7d9f4c6b8-xkp2m

# Step 1: list Pods and Services

# Step 2: describe one Pod

# Step 3: follow the Pod's logs
tasks.solution:
# Step 1
kubectl get pods
kubectl get svc

# Step 2  (substitute a real pod name from the output above)
kubectl describe pod <pod-name>

# Step 3
kubectl logs -f <pod-name>
tasks.explanation: `kubectl get` gives you a quick summary table — enough to spot which Pod is unhealthy. `kubectl describe pod` prints the full object spec plus a chronological Events list at the bottom; Events are the single most informative debugging output in Kubernetes because they record scheduler decisions, image pulls, probe failures, and restart reasons. `kubectl logs -f` tails the container's stdout/stderr in real time, which is where application-level errors (tracebacks, port conflicts, missing env vars) appear. The three commands cover three different layers: cluster inventory, Kubernetes-layer events, and application output.
hints: Copy a Pod name from the `kubectl get pods` output; the name looks like `flask-capstone-<hash>-<hash>`.
In the `describe` output, scroll to the very bottom — the `Events:` section is last, and it is where Kubernetes records what it actually did (pull, start, liveness/readiness probe results).
Use `kubectl logs --previous <pod-name>` if the Pod has already crashed and restarted; it shows the log from the terminated container rather than the current running one.

tasks.prompt: Open a port-forward tunnel from `localhost:8080` to the `flask-capstone` Service on port 5000, then verify the app is reachable by curling both the root path and the `/healthz` endpoint.
tasks.starter:
# Terminal A — start the tunnel (leave this running)

# Terminal B — send requests while the tunnel is open
tasks.solution:
# Terminal A
kubectl port-forward svc/flask-capstone 8080:5000

# Terminal B
curl http://localhost:8080/
curl http://localhost:8080/healthz
tasks.explanation: `kubectl port-forward svc/<name> <local-port>:<service-port>` creates an authenticated tunnel through the Kubernetes API server; it requires no external LoadBalancer or open firewall rule, making it ideal for quick interactive testing. The tunnel exists only while the command runs — Ctrl-C tears it down. A `200` response from `/healthz` confirms that the Pod is not just started but is genuinely serving HTTP traffic, which is the definition of a healthy deployment.
hints: Run the `port-forward` command in a dedicated terminal tab so you can leave it running while you curl from another tab.
If `curl` returns `connection refused`, check that the port-forward process is still running and that you are curling port `8080`, not `5000`.
On minikube you can also use `minikube service flask-capstone --url` as a more durable alternative — it prints a URL backed by a NodePort that survives without an open terminal.

tasks.prompt: Perform a rolling update to `flask-capstone:2.0` using `kubectl set image`, watch the rollout progress with `kubectl rollout status`, and observe old Pods being replaced by new ones in parallel with `kubectl get pods -w`.
tasks.starter:
# Assume flask-capstone:2.0 has already been built and loaded into the cluster:
#   minikube image load flask-capstone:2.0

# Terminal A — trigger the update

# Terminal B — watch rollout status (blocks until complete)

# Terminal C (optional) — watch Pod replacement live
tasks.solution:
# Terminal A
kubectl set image deployment/flask-capstone flask-capstone=flask-capstone:2.0

# Terminal B
kubectl rollout status deployment/flask-capstone

# Terminal C (optional)
kubectl get pods -w
tasks.explanation: `kubectl set image deployment/<name> <container>=<image>` is an imperative shortcut that patches the container's image field inside the Deployment spec. Kubernetes detects the change and starts a rolling update: it creates new-version Pods (capped by `maxSurge`, default 25%) and terminates old ones only as replacements become Ready (governed by `maxUnavailable`, also default 25%), so at least 75% of desired capacity is always serving traffic. `kubectl rollout status` blocks and prints human-readable progress, finishing with `successfully rolled out`. The `-w` watch on `get pods` lets you see the old `flask-capstone-*` Pods reach `Terminating` while new ones start up — the visual proof of zero-downtime rolling replacement.
hints: The `set image` syntax is `deployment/<deployment-name> <container-name>=<new-image>`. The container name is the `name:` field inside the `containers:` list in your deployment.yaml — in this capstone it is `flask-capstone`.

If `rollout status` hangs for more than two minutes, the new Pods are probably unhealthy. Open another terminal and run `kubectl get pods` to see if any are in `ImagePullBackOff` or `CrashLoopBackOff`.
You can also edit `deployment.yaml` to change the image tag to `flask-capstone:2.0` and re-run `kubectl apply -f deployment.yaml` — both approaches trigger the identical rolling update mechanism.

tasks.prompt: Simulate a bad deploy by updating the Deployment to the non-existent tag `flask-capstone:broken`, observe that `ImagePullBackOff` appears while old Pods keep running (no downtime), then roll back to the previous revision with a single command and confirm the rollback succeeds.
tasks.starter:
# Step 1: trigger a bad update

# Step 2: observe the failure (what status do the new Pods show?)

# Step 3: confirm old Pods are still Running and serving

# Step 4: roll back

# Step 5: verify the rollback completed successfully
tasks.solution:
# Step 1: trigger the bad update
kubectl set image deployment/flask-capstone flask-capstone=flask-capstone:broken

# Step 2: watch new Pods fail to pull the image
kubectl get pods -w
# New pods will show ImagePullBackOff; old pods stay Running

# Step 3: confirm the app is still reachable on v2 (or v1 if rolling back from v1)
# (keep port-forward running in another terminal)
curl http://localhost:8080/healthz

# Step 4: roll back to the previous revision
kubectl rollout undo deployment/flask-capstone

# Step 5: verify
kubectl rollout status deployment/flask-capstone
kubectl get pods
tasks.explanation: This exercise demonstrates the Kubernetes safety net that makes rolling updates trustworthy in production. Because the Deployment's `maxUnavailable` is set to 25% (default), Kubernetes will not terminate more old-version Pods than the update allows healthy new ones to replace. Since `flask-capstone:broken` cannot be pulled, no new Pod ever becomes Ready, so no old Pod is terminated — the old ReplicaSet continues to serve all traffic. `kubectl rollout undo` reverses the Deployment to its previous revision (stored in the rollout history) using the same gradual mechanism, just in the opposite direction. The result: a bad image tag causes a hanging rollout and never a service outage. `kubectl rollout history deployment/flask-capstone` lists all numbered revisions; you can target any specific one with `--to-revision=<N>`.
hints: Run `kubectl describe pod <new-pod-name>` on one of the stuck new Pods — the Events section will show `Failed to pull image` with the exact error, which is how you confirm `ImagePullBackOff` in a real incident.
While the bad rollout is hanging, curl your service to prove the old Pods are still answering; this is the key observation that shows zero-downtime safety.
After `rollout undo`, run `kubectl rollout status` and wait for `successfully rolled out` before moving on — the rollback is itself a rolling operation and takes a few seconds.
